title: Gaussian Elimination Algorithm
description: Algorithm to solve system of linear equations by transforming augmented matrix to row echelon form
code:
ALGORITHM GaussianElimination(AugmentedMatrix[1..m][1..n+1])
BEGIN
 // m = number of equations, n = number of variables
 // Last column (n+1) contains constants
 
 row <- 1
 FOR col <- 1 TO n DO
 // Find pivot in current column
 maxRow <- row
 FOR i <- row+1 TO m DO
 IF |AugmentedMatrix[i][col]| > |AugmentedMatrix[maxRow][col]| THEN
 maxRow <- i
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Swap rows if needed
 IF maxRow ≠ row THEN
 SWAP(AugmentedMatrix[row], AugmentedMatrix[maxRow])
 END IF
 
 // Make pivot = 1 (optional for Gaussian)
 pivot <- AugmentedMatrix[row][col]
 IF pivot ≠ 0 THEN
 FOR j <- col TO n+1 DO
 AugmentedMatrix[row][j] <- AugmentedMatrix[row][j] / pivot
 END FOR
 END IF
 
 // Eliminate below
 FOR i <- row+1 TO m DO
 factor <- AugmentedMatrix[i][col]
 FOR j <- col TO n+1 DO
 AugmentedMatrix[i][j] <- AugmentedMatrix[i][j] - factor * AugmentedMatrix[row][j]
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 row <- row + 1
 END FOR
 
 // Back-substitution to find solution
 solution[n] <- AugmentedMatrix[n][n+1]
 FOR i <- n-1 DOWNTO 1 DO
 sum <- 0
 FOR j <- i+1 TO n DO
 sum <- sum + AugmentedMatrix[i][j] * solution[j]
 END FOR
 solution[i] <- (AugmentedMatrix[i][n+1] - sum) / AugmentedMatrix[i][i]
 END FOR
 
 RETURN solution
END
explanation: Gaussian elimination converts the augmented matrix to upper triangular form (REF), then uses back-substitution to find each variable. The forward elimination zeroes out elements below pivots, while back-substitution solves from the last equation upward.

title: Gauss-Jordan Elimination Algorithm
description: Algorithm to solve systems by transforming directly to reduced row echelon form
code:
ALGORITHM GaussJordan(AugmentedMatrix[1..m][1..n+1])
BEGIN
 row <- 1
 FOR col <- 1 TO n DO
 // Find pivot
 maxRow <- row
 FOR i <- row TO m DO
 IF |AugmentedMatrix[i][col]| > |AugmentedMatrix[maxRow][col]| THEN
 maxRow <- i
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Swap rows
 IF maxRow ≠ row THEN
 SWAP(AugmentedMatrix[row], AugmentedMatrix[maxRow])
 END IF
 
 // Skip if pivot is zero (column is dependent)
 IF AugmentedMatrix[row][col] = 0 THEN
 CONTINUE
 END IF
 
 // Scale pivot row to make leading entry 1
 FOR j <- col TO n+1 DO
 AugmentedMatrix[row][j] <- AugmentedMatrix[row][j] / AugmentedMatrix[row][col]
 END FOR
 
 // Eliminate ALL other entries in pivot column
 FOR i <- 1 TO m DO
 IF i ≠ row THEN
 factor <- AugmentedMatrix[i][col]
 FOR j <- col TO n+1 DO
 AugmentedMatrix[i][j] <- AugmentedMatrix[i][j] - factor * AugmentedMatrix[row][j]
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 row <- row + 1
 END FOR
 
 // Solution is now in last column!
 RETURN AugmentedMatrix[1..n][n+1]
END
explanation: Gauss-Jordan eliminates entries both above and below each pivot, reaching RREF directly. This gives the solution without back-substitution - each row shows the value of its corresponding variable.
